title: CXR — Approach
oneLiner: Systematic interpretation of chest X-ray to identify life-threatening pathologies (tension PTX, massive haemothorax, tamponade) and common diagnoses in the acutely unwell patient.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax:** Look for **tracheal deviation away** from the collapsed lung + hyperlucent hemithorax + flattened diaphragm. Single finding: **tracheal deviation** — if present and patient unstable, decompress immediately before seeking CT.
- **Massive haemothorax:** Opacification of one hemithorax with **contralateral mediastinal shift** (shift *away* from opacity). Single finding: **blunted costophrenic angle + mediastinal shift** — if trauma, insert large-bore chest drain.
- **Cardiac tamponade:** Enlarged **globular cardiac silhouette** with **clear lung fields** (water bottle sign). Single finding: **globular heart without pulmonary congestion** — call echo immediately if suspected.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms** prompting CXR: dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, cough + fever, haemoptysis, post-procedure (e.g., line insertion, thoracentesis), pre-operative assessment.
- **Key exam findings:** reduced air entry, dullness/hyperresonance on percussion, tracheal deviation, stony dullness, crackles/wheeze, oxygen saturation <94% on room air.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
*Based on CXR pattern (not symptom)*

- **Airspace opacification (consolidation):** Pneumonia (focal, air bronchograms) vs pulmonary oedema (diffuse, batwing, Kerley B lines) vs contusion (trauma) vs atelectasis (volume loss, fissure displacement)
- **Interstitial pattern (reticular/nodular):** ILD (UIP, NSIP) vs pulmonary oedema (septal lines) vs infection (viral, PCP) vs malignancy (lymphangitic carcinomatosis)
- **Pleural effusion:** Transudate (CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic) vs exudate (infection, malignancy, PE)
- **Pneumothorax:** Spontaneous (primary vs secondary) vs iatrogenic (post line/ventilation) vs tension (if mediastinal shift)
- **Mediastinal widening:** Aortic dissection (loss of aortic knuckle, tracheal deviation) vs large hiatus hernia vs lymphadenopathy (sarcoid, TB, lymphoma)
- **Splitter:** *Kerley B lines + batwing distribution* → pulmonary oedema (not consolidation). If absent and focal → pneumonia.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Review previous CXR, check clinical context (trauma? ventilation? central line?), measure respiratory rate, SpO2, blood pressure.
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, U&E, lactate, D-dimer (if PE suspected), troponin (if cardiac), blood cultures (if infection).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR (AP supine or PA erect) – always assess technical quality: rotation, inspiration, penetration.
  - **Second-line:** CT chest (with contrast if PE/aortic dissection; high-resolution for ILD; low-dose for follow-up nodules).
  - **Lung ultrasound:** For pleural effusion, pneumothorax, consolidation (bedside, no radiation).
- **Special tests:** Pleural aspiration for effusion (Light’s criteria, culture, cytology), bronchoscopy for persistent collapse/consolidation.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Not applicable for CXR itself; use scoring systems for specific conditions (e.g., CURB-65 for pneumonia, Wells for PE – but CXR is one component).
- **Never-miss test:** **Comparison with previous CXR** – the single most common omission that leads to missing slow-growing malignancy or misinterpreting chronic changes as acute.
sections.Management:
*Management depends on the CXR finding; this is the approach to acting on the result.*

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Tension pneumothorax → needle decompression (14G cannula, 2nd ICS midclavicular line) then chest drain.
  - Massive haemothorax → large-bore chest drain (intercostal drain 28–32Fr, connected to underwater seal + suction).
  - Cardiac tamponade → pericardiocentesis (echo-guided) or surgical window; call cardiothoracic.
  - Suspicion of aortic dissection → urgent CT aortogram, IV metoprolol (5 mg IV, repeat every 5 min to HR <60), call vascular/cardiothoracic.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Pneumonia → start empiric antibiotics per local guidelines (e.g., amoxicillin 500–1000 mg PO TDS for 5 days, or doxycycline if penicillin allergy); review CURB-65.
  - Pulmonary oedema → diuresis (furosemide 40–80 mg IV, monitor urine output), treat underlying cause (ACS, valve disease, arrhythmia).
  - Pleural effusion → diagnostic tap; if symptomatic, consider therapeutic aspiration/drain.
  - Interstitial pattern → if acute: consider PCP (corticosteroids + co-trimoxazole), if chronic: refer respiratory for HRCT.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Incidental nodule → follow Fleischner Society guidelines for CT surveillance.
  - Chronic interstitial changes → pulmonary function tests, rheumatology/ILD clinic.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV maintenance if NBM (e.g., 1 L Plasmalyte/5% dextrose over 8h) – adjust for CHF/oedema.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (contraindication: active bleeding, coagulation disorder).
  - Diet/NBM: NBM if immediate intervention anticipated (e.g., pericardiocentesis, chest drain, OGD for foreign body?).
  - Observations: Minimum 4-hourly NEWS2; more frequent if unstable (hourly until CXR reviewed).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **For tension pneumothorax:** No drug – procedure.
- **For pneumonia (CAP):** Amoxicillin 500 mg–1 g PO TDS × 5 days; if severe (CURB-65 ≥3): Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS + clarithromycin 500 mg IV BD.
- **For pulmonary oedema:** Furosemide 40–80 mg IV (max 120 mg stat); monitor K+.
- **For pleural infection:** Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS + metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS.
- **Key contraindications:** Penicillin allergy → use doxycycline or levofloxacin (check local policy).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Furosemide may accumulate in renal impairment; monitor U&E. Amoxicillin dose reduce if eGFR <30 (see BNF).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Furosemide – giving too large a bolus (e.g., >80 mg) in undiagnosed aortic stenosis or hypovolaemic patient → hypotension, renal failure.** Always check BP and JVP.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Missed tension pneumothorax (death), misinterpreted infection (delayed antibiotics for empyema), overdiagnosis of pulmonary oedema (missing PE with atelectasis), procedure complications (haemorrhage, pneumothorax from chest drain).
- **Chronic:** Missing malignancy on interval comparison (e.g., slowly growing nodule), radiation overuse (repeated CTs in young patients).
sections.Prognosis: - CXR itself has no prognosis – the underlying condition dictates outcome. Timely interpretation reduces morbidity in pneumonia (antibiotics <4h), PE (anticoagulation), and pneumothorax (decompression). Incidental nodules: 5-year survival >95% if stage I lung cancer resected early.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always compare to old CXR** – the single best discriminator between acute and chronic.
- **Use the ABCDE method** (Airway, Breathing, Cardiac, Diaphragm, Extras) to avoid missing findings.
- **Look for the "hidden" abnormalities:** retrocardiac pneumonia, behind the heart, hila, apices (Pancoast tumour), and under the diaphragm (free air → perforated viscus).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Interpreting an AP supine CXR as erect** – mediastinal widening is normal; do not diagnose cardiomegaly or pneumothorax without upright film.
- **Hearing "clear CXR" and stopping** – up to 20% of PE and early pneumonia have normal X-rays; always correlate clinically.
- **Ignoring rotation** – a rotated film can mimic mediastinal shift; check clavicles symmetric against spinous processes.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Increasing dyspnoea, haemoptysis, fever not improving after 48h on antibiotics, new pleuritic pain.
- **Follow-up:** If incidental nodule: CT surveillance per Fleischner protocol (3–12 months). If pneumonia: clinical review in 6 weeks with repeat CXR to confirm resolution (especially if smoker or age >50).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5, SpO2 <90% despite 15 L O2, respiratory rate >25, systolic BP <90, lactate >2, GCS <15, or sign of tamponade/tension PTX.
- **Specific thresholds:** Tension pneumothorax → immediate decompression before calling. Aortic dissection → call cardiothoracic. Massive haemothorax → call cardiothoracic if >1.5 L initial drainage or ongoing bleeding.
- **Crash team:** If unconscious, pulseless, or respiratory arrest – call 2222 (UK).

rawMarkdown:
## CXR — Approach
**One-liner:** Systematic interpretation of chest X-ray to identify life-threatening pathologies (tension PTX, massive haemothorax, tamponade) and common diagnoses in the acutely unwell patient.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax:** Look for **tracheal deviation away** from the collapsed lung + hyperlucent hemithorax + flattened diaphragm. Single finding: **tracheal deviation** — if present and patient unstable, decompress immediately before seeking CT.
- **Massive haemothorax:** Opacification of one hemithorax with **contralateral mediastinal shift** (shift *away* from opacity). Single finding: **blunted costophrenic angle + mediastinal shift** — if trauma, insert large-bore chest drain.
- **Cardiac tamponade:** Enlarged **globular cardiac silhouette** with **clear lung fields** (water bottle sign). Single finding: **globular heart without pulmonary congestion** — call echo immediately if suspected.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms** prompting CXR: dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, cough + fever, haemoptysis, post-procedure (e.g., line insertion, thoracentesis), pre-operative assessment.
- **Key exam findings:** reduced air entry, dullness/hyperresonance on percussion, tracheal deviation, stony dullness, crackles/wheeze, oxygen saturation <94% on room air.

### Differential Diagnosis
*Based on CXR pattern (not symptom)*

- **Airspace opacification (consolidation):** Pneumonia (focal, air bronchograms) vs pulmonary oedema (diffuse, batwing, Kerley B lines) vs contusion (trauma) vs atelectasis (volume loss, fissure displacement)
- **Interstitial pattern (reticular/nodular):** ILD (UIP, NSIP) vs pulmonary oedema (septal lines) vs infection (viral, PCP) vs malignancy (lymphangitic carcinomatosis)
- **Pleural effusion:** Transudate (CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic) vs exudate (infection, malignancy, PE)
- **Pneumothorax:** Spontaneous (primary vs secondary) vs iatrogenic (post line/ventilation) vs tension (if mediastinal shift)
- **Mediastinal widening:** Aortic dissection (loss of aortic knuckle, tracheal deviation) vs large hiatus hernia vs lymphadenopathy (sarcoid, TB, lymphoma)
- **Splitter:** *Kerley B lines + batwing distribution* → pulmonary oedema (not consolidation). If absent and focal → pneumonia.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Review previous CXR, check clinical context (trauma? ventilation? central line?), measure respiratory rate, SpO2, blood pressure.
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, U&E, lactate, D-dimer (if PE suspected), troponin (if cardiac), blood cultures (if infection).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR (AP supine or PA erect) – always assess technical quality: rotation, inspiration, penetration.
  - **Second-line:** CT chest (with contrast if PE/aortic dissection; high-resolution for ILD; low-dose for follow-up nodules).
  - **Lung ultrasound:** For pleural effusion, pneumothorax, consolidation (bedside, no radiation).
- **Special tests:** Pleural aspiration for effusion (Light’s criteria, culture, cytology), bronchoscopy for persistent collapse/consolidation.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Not applicable for CXR itself; use scoring systems for specific conditions (e.g., CURB-65 for pneumonia, Wells for PE – but CXR is one component).
- **Never-miss test:** **Comparison with previous CXR** – the single most common omission that leads to missing slow-growing malignancy or misinterpreting chronic changes as acute.

### Management
*Management depends on the CXR finding; this is the approach to acting on the result.*

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Tension pneumothorax → needle decompression (14G cannula, 2nd ICS midclavicular line) then chest drain.
  - Massive haemothorax → large-bore chest drain (intercostal drain 28–32Fr, connected to underwater seal + suction).
  - Cardiac tamponade → pericardiocentesis (echo-guided) or surgical window; call cardiothoracic.
  - Suspicion of aortic dissection → urgent CT aortogram, IV metoprolol (5 mg IV, repeat every 5 min to HR <60), call vascular/cardiothoracic.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Pneumonia → start empiric antibiotics per local guidelines (e.g., amoxicillin 500–1000 mg PO TDS for 5 days, or doxycycline if penicillin allergy); review CURB-65.
  - Pulmonary oedema → diuresis (furosemide 40–80 mg IV, monitor urine output), treat underlying cause (ACS, valve disease, arrhythmia).
  - Pleural effusion → diagnostic tap; if symptomatic, consider therapeutic aspiration/drain.
  - Interstitial pattern → if acute: consider PCP (corticosteroids + co-trimoxazole), if chronic: refer respiratory for HRCT.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Incidental nodule → follow Fleischner Society guidelines for CT surveillance.
  - Chronic interstitial changes → pulmonary function tests, rheumatology/ILD clinic.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV maintenance if NBM (e.g., 1 L Plasmalyte/5% dextrose over 8h) – adjust for CHF/oedema.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (contraindication: active bleeding, coagulation disorder).
  - Diet/NBM: NBM if immediate intervention anticipated (e.g., pericardiocentesis, chest drain, OGD for foreign body?).
  - Observations: Minimum 4-hourly NEWS2; more frequent if unstable (hourly until CXR reviewed).

### Drugs & Doses
- **For tension pneumothorax:** No drug – procedure.
- **For pneumonia (CAP):** Amoxicillin 500 mg–1 g PO TDS × 5 days; if severe (CURB-65 ≥3): Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS + clarithromycin 500 mg IV BD.
- **For pulmonary oedema:** Furosemide 40–80 mg IV (max 120 mg stat); monitor K+.
- **For pleural infection:** Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS + metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS.
- **Key contraindications:** Penicillin allergy → use doxycycline or levofloxacin (check local policy).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Furosemide may accumulate in renal impairment; monitor U&E. Amoxicillin dose reduce if eGFR <30 (see BNF).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Furosemide – giving too large a bolus (e.g., >80 mg) in undiagnosed aortic stenosis or hypovolaemic patient → hypotension, renal failure.** Always check BP and JVP.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Missed tension pneumothorax (death), misinterpreted infection (delayed antibiotics for empyema), overdiagnosis of pulmonary oedema (missing PE with atelectasis), procedure complications (haemorrhage, pneumothorax from chest drain).
- **Chronic:** Missing malignancy on interval comparison (e.g., slowly growing nodule), radiation overuse (repeated CTs in young patients).

### Prognosis
- CXR itself has no prognosis – the underlying condition dictates outcome. Timely interpretation reduces morbidity in pneumonia (antibiotics <4h), PE (anticoagulation), and pneumothorax (decompression). Incidental nodules: 5-year survival >95% if stage I lung cancer resected early.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always compare to old CXR** – the single best discriminator between acute and chronic.
- **Use the ABCDE method** (Airway, Breathing, Cardiac, Diaphragm, Extras) to avoid missing findings.
- **Look for the "hidden" abnormalities:** retrocardiac pneumonia, behind the heart, hila, apices (Pancoast tumour), and under the diaphragm (free air → perforated viscus).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Interpreting an AP supine CXR as erect** – mediastinal widening is normal; do not diagnose cardiomegaly or pneumothorax without upright film.
- **Hearing "clear CXR" and stopping** – up to 20% of PE and early pneumonia have normal X-rays; always correlate clinically.
- **Ignoring rotation** – a rotated film can mimic mediastinal shift; check clavicles symmetric against spinous processes.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Increasing dyspnoea, haemoptysis, fever not improving after 48h on antibiotics, new pleuritic pain.
- **Follow-up:** If incidental nodule: CT surveillance per Fleischner protocol (3–12 months). If pneumonia: clinical review in 6 weeks with repeat CXR to confirm resolution (especially if smoker or age >50).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5, SpO2 <90% despite 15 L O2, respiratory rate >25, systolic BP <90, lactate >2, GCS <15, or sign of tamponade/tension PTX.
- **Specific thresholds:** Tension pneumothorax → immediate decompression before calling. Aortic dissection → call cardiothoracic. Massive haemothorax → call cardiothoracic if >1.5 L initial drainage or ongoing bleeding.
- **Crash team:** If unconscious, pulseless, or respiratory arrest – call 2222 (UK).
